---
title: "Class 18: Pertussis Mini Project"
author: "Nathan Joseph (PID:A17668656)"
format: pdf
toc: TRUE
---

## Background
Pertussis (a.k.a. whooping cough) is a common lung infection caused by the bacteria B. Pertussis.

This can affect all ages but is most severe for those under 1 year of age.

The CDC tracks the number of [reported cases in the US](https://www.cdc.gov/pertussis/php/surveillance/pertussis-cases-by-year.html?CDC_AAref_Val=https://www.cdc.gov/pertussis/surv-reporting/cases-by-year.html):

We can "scrape" this data with the **datapasta** package.

```{r}
cdc <- data.frame(
  year = c(
    1922L,
    1923L,
    1924L,
    1925L,
    1926L,
    1927L,
    1928L,
    1929L,
    1930L,
    1931L,
    1932L,
    1933L,
    1934L,
    1935L,
    1936L,
    1937L,
    1938L,
    1939L,
    1940L,
    1941L,
    1942L,
    1943L,
    1944L,
    1945L,
    1946L,
    1947L,
    1948L,
    1949L,
    1950L,
    1951L,
    1952L,
    1953L,
    1954L,
    1955L,
    1956L,
    1957L,
    1958L,
    1959L,
    1960L,
    1961L,
    1962L,
    1963L,
    1964L,
    1965L,
    1966L,
    1967L,
    1968L,
    1969L,
    1970L,
    1971L,
    1972L,
    1973L,
    1974L,
    1975L,
    1976L,
    1977L,
    1978L,
    1979L,
    1980L,
    1981L,
    1982L,
    1983L,
    1984L,
    1985L,
    1986L,
    1987L,
    1988L,
    1989L,
    1990L,
    1991L,
    1992L,
    1993L,
    1994L,
    1995L,
    1996L,
    1997L,
    1998L,
    1999L,
    2000L,
    2001L,
    2002L,
    2003L,
    2004L,
    2005L,
    2006L,
    2007L,
    2008L,
    2009L,
    2010L,
    2011L,
    2012L,
    2013L,
    2014L,
    2015L,
    2016L,
    2017L,
    2018L,
    2019L,
    2020L,
    2021L,
    2022L,
    2023L,
    2024L,
    2025L
  ),
  cases = c(
    107473,
    164191,
    165418,
    152003,
    202210,
    181411,
    161799,
    197371,
    166914,
    172559,
    215343,
    179135,
    265269,
    180518,
    147237,
    214652,
    227319,
    103188,
    183866,
    222202,
    191383,
    191890,
    109873,
    133792,
    109860,
    156517,
    74715,
    69479,
    120718,
    68687,
    45030,
    37129,
    60886,
    62786,
    31732,
    28295,
    32148,
    40005,
    14809,
    11468,
    17749,
    17135,
    13005,
    6799,
    7717,
    9718,
    4810,
    3285,
    4249,
    3036,
    3287,
    1759,
    2402,
    1738,
    1010,
    2177,
    2063,
    1623,
    1730,
    1248,
    1895,
    2463,
    2276,
    3589,
    4195,
    2823,
    3450,
    4157,
    4570,
    2719,
    4083,
    6586,
    4617,
    5137,
    7796,
    6564,
    7405,
    7298,
    7867,
    7580,
    9771,
    11647,
    25827,
    25616,
    15632,
    10454,
    13278,
    16858,
    27550,
    18719,
    48277,
    28639,
    32971,
    20762,
    17972,
    18975,
    15609,
    18617,
    6124,
    2116,
    3044,
    7063,
    22538,
    21996
  )
)
```

> Q. Make a plot of `year` vs `cases`

```{r}
library(ggplot2)
ggplot(cdc) +
  aes(year, cases) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_line() 
```

> Q. Add some major milestones including the first wP vaccine roll-out (1946), the switch to the newer aP vaccine (of 1996), the COVID years (2020)?

```{r}
ggplot(cdc) +
  aes(year, cases) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_line() +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 1946, col="blue", lty=2) + 
  geom_vline(xintercept = 1996, col="red", lty=2) + 
  geom_vline(xintercept = 2020, col="gray", lty=2)
```

There were high case numbers in the pre-1940s, then the introduction of the first wP vaccine in 1946 lowered the case numbers and then the case numbers got even lower with the introduction of the second aP vaccine in 1996. However in the mid-2000s, the case numbers increased due to circulating myths regarding vaccines and more people lacking the booster update shots but the case numbers lowered in 2020 due to quarantine and lockdown mandates which prevented transmission of the whooping cough.

**Why is this vaccine-preventable disease on the upswing?** To answer this question we need to investigate the mechanisms underlying waning protection against pertussis. This requires evaluation of pertussis-specific immune responses over time in wP and aP vaccinated individuals.

## CMI-PB Project
[Computational Models of Immunity - Pertussis Boost](https://www.cmi-pb.org/) project aims to provide the scientific community with this very information.

They make their data available vis JSON format returning API. We can read this in R with the `read_json()` function from the **jsonlite** package: 

```{r}
library(jsonlite)

subject <- read_json("https://www.cmi-pb.org/api/subject", simplifyVector = TRUE)

head(subject)
```

> Q. How many wP and aP individuals are in this `subject` table?

```{r}
sum(subject$infancy_vac == "wP")
sum(subject$infancy_vac == "aP")
```

```{r}
table(subject$infancy_vac)
```

> Q. What is the biological sex breakdown?

```{r}
table(subject$biological_sex)
```

> Q. In terms of race and gender, is this dataset representative of the US population?

```{r}
table(subject$race, subject$biological_sex)
```

Let's read some more tables:

```{r}
specimen <- read_json("http://cmi-pb.org/api/v5_1/specimen", simplifyVector = T)

ab_titer <- read_json("http://cmi-pb.org/api/v5_1/plasma_ab_titer", simplifyVector = T)
```

```{r}
head(specimen)
```

```{r}
head(ab_titer)
```

To analyze this data, we need to first "join" (merge/link) the different tables so we have all the data in one place not spread across different tables.

We can use the `*_join()` family of functions from **dplyr** to do this

```{r}
library(dplyr)
meta <- inner_join(subject, specimen)
head(meta)
```

```{r}
abdata <- inner_join(ab_titer, meta)
head(abdata)
```

> Q. What antibody isotypes are measured for these patients?

```{r}
table(ab_titer$isotype)
```

> Q. What antigens are reported?

```{r}
table(abdata$antigen)
```

Let's focus on the IgG isotype and make a plot of MFI_normalized for all antigens

```{r}
igg <- abdata |>
  filter(isotype == "IgG")

head(igg)
```

```{r}
ggplot(igg) +
  aes(MFI_normalised, antigen) +
  geom_boxplot()
```

> Q. Is there a difference for aP vs wP individuals with these values?

```{r}
ggplot(igg) +
  aes(MFI_normalised, antigen) +
  geom_boxplot() + 
  facet_wrap(~infancy_vac)
```

```{r}
ggplot(igg) +
  aes(MFI_normalised, antigen, col = infancy_vac) +
  geom_boxplot()
```

> Q. Is there a temporal response - i.e. do values increase or decrease over time?

```{r}
ggplot(igg) +
  aes(MFI_normalised, antigen, col = infancy_vac) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  facet_wrap(~visit)
```

## Focus on "PT" Pertussis Toxin antigen

```{r}
pt.igg.21 <- igg |> filter(antigen == "PT",
              dataset == "2021_dataset")
```

```{r}
ggplot(pt.igg.21) +
  aes(planned_day_relative_to_boost, 
      MFI_normalised, 
      col = infancy_vac,
      group = subject_id) + 
  geom_point() + 
  geom_line() +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 14, lty=2) + 
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0, lty=2)
```

```{r}
ggplot(pt.igg.21) +
  aes(planned_day_relative_to_boost, 
      MFI_normalised, 
      col = infancy_vac,
      group = subject_id) + 
  geom_point() + 
  geom_line(alpha = 0.3) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 14, lty=2) + 
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0, lty=2) +
  geom_smooth(aes(group = infancy_vac), se = FALSE, size = 1.2,
              span = 0.75, method.args = list(degree = 1)) + 
  labs(
    title = "CMI-PB 2021 Dataset IgG PT",
    subtitle = "Dashed lines at day 0 (pre boost) and day 14 (post boost)",
    x = "Day Relative to Boost",
    y = "Normalized MFI"
  )
```
